perturbation.original_text: The term “Affiliate” shall have the meaning provided such term in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Act, as amended (the “ 1934 Act ”) and, for avoidance of doubt, includes your subsidiaries and Affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “Affiliate” shall mean any entity controlling, controlled by, or under common control with a party, excluding any entity registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding entities registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 from the definition of 'Affiliate,' the contract creates a legal contradiction. The Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which usually provides the standard definition, does not inherently exclude such entities.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the broad definition of 'affiliate' commonly understood under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, potentially misclassifying entities and affecting obligations related to securities transactions.

Definitions and application (a) Definitions When used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires- (1) The term "exchange" means any organization, association, or group of persons, whether incorporated or unincorporated, which constitutes, maintains, or provides a market place or facilities for bringing together purchasers and sellers of securities or for otherwise performing wi
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 by excluding entities registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 from the definition of 'Affiliate'. The original text explicitly refers to the definition of 'Affiliate' as provided in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The modified text creates a narrower definition, potentially misclassifying entities and affecting obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 USC 78a), indicating its broad scope and applicability. The act's definitions are undermined by the exclusion introduced in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: If you or any of the Representatives are requested or required (in the opinion of your counsel) by order of court, legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand, a governmental agency, a stock exchange or other similar process to disclose any of the Evaluation Material or any of the facts, disclosure of which is prohibited under this Confidentiality Agreement, you will provide the Company with prompt written notice of any such requests or requirements together with copies of the material proposed to be disclosed so that the Company may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If you or any of the Representatives are requested or required by a verbal request from a governmental employee to disclose any of the Evaluation Material or any of the facts, disclosure of which is prohibited under this Confidentiality Agreement, you will have no obligation to notify the company.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the notification requirement for verbal requests, creating a contradiction. Legally, a verbal request from a governmental agency may still carry legal weight depending on the circumstances and can trigger disclosure obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Administrative Procedure Act outlines requirements for agency actions, including requests for information. While not explicitly requiring notification for *all* requests, removing the obligation for *any* verbal request contradicts the general expectation of due process and notification, potentially hindering the company's ability to protect its confidential information when a verbal request is legally significant and should be documented.

Ancillary matters (a) This section applies, according to the provisions thereof, except as otherwise provided by this subchapter. (b) A person compelled to appear in person before an agency or representative thereof is entitled to be accompanied, represented, and advised by counsel or, if permitted by the agency, by other qualified representative. A party is entitled to appear in person or by
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 5 U.S. Code § 555 - Ancillary matters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 5 U.S. Code § 555 - Ancillary matters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) This section applies, according to the provisions thereof, except as otherwise provided by this subchapter. (b) A person compelled to appear in person before an agency or representative thereof is entitled to be accompanied, represented, and advised by counsel or, if permitted by the agency, by other qualified representative. A party is entitled to appear in person or by or with counsel or other duly qualified representative in an agency proceeding . So far as the orderly conduct of public business permits, an interested person may appear before an agency or its responsible employees for the presentation, adjustment, or determination of an issue, request, or controversy in a proceeding, whether interlocutory, summary, or otherwise, or in connection with an agency function. With due regard for the convenience and necessity of the parties or their representatives and within a reasonable time, each agency shall proceed to conclude a matter presented to it. This subsection does not grant or deny a person who is not a lawyer the right to appear for or represent others before an agency or in an agency proceeding . (c) Process, requirement of a report, inspection, or other investigative act or demand may not be issued, made, or enforced except as authorized by law. A person compelled to submit data or evidence is entitled to retain or, on payment of lawfully prescribed costs, procure a copy or transcript thereof, except that in a nonpublic investigatory proceeding the witness may for good cause be limited to inspection of the official transcript of his testimony. (d) Agency subpenas authorized by law shall be issued to a party on request and, when required by rules of procedure, on a statement or showing of general rele
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the notification requirement for verbal requests from governmental employees, which contradicts the original text's obligation to notify the company of any request from a governmental agency. While 5 U.S. Code § 555 doesn't explicitly mandate notification for all requests, it addresses the rights of individuals dealing with agencies. Specifically, subsection (c) states that investigative demands may not be enforced except as authorized by law, and individuals compelled to submit data are entitled to a copy of it. Removing the notification requirement hinders the company's ability to ensure the verbal request is authorized by law and to obtain a copy of any submitted data, undermining the protections afforded by § 555(c).

perturbation.original_text: You understand and agree that no contract or agreement providing for a Transaction shall be deemed to exist between you and the Company unless and until you and the Company execute and deliver a final definitive agreement relating to a Transaction (a “ Transaction Agreement ”), and you hereby waive, in advance, any claims (including, without limitation, breach of contract) relating to the existence of a Transaction unless and until you and the Company shall have executed and delivered a Transaction Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: You understand and agree that no contract or agreement providing for a Transaction shall be deemed to exist between you and the Company unless and until you and the Company execute and deliver a preliminary agreement. You hereby waive, in advance, any claims (including, without limitation, breach of contract) relating to the existence of a Transaction unless and until you and the Company shall have executed and delivered a preliminary agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'final definitive agreement' to 'preliminary agreement' creates a contradiction. A preliminary agreement usually does not bind the parties in its entirety, unlike a final agreement. This introduces uncertainty as to when a binding agreement comes into effect.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - general principles of offer and acceptance
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts established contract law principles where the intention to be bound and the definiteness of terms are key to forming a binding agreement. A 'preliminary agreement' suggests a lack of finality and commitment, conflicting with the usual requirements for a valid contract.

perturbation.justification: The modification replaces 'final definitive agreement' with 'preliminary agreement,' weakening the commitment to a binding transaction. The Law Explanation states this contradicts contract law principles, where 'the intention to be bound and the definiteness of terms are key to forming a binding agreement.' A 'preliminary agreement' lacks finality, contrasting the requirements for a valid contract. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. While the scraped snippets provide no direct support, the Perturbation and Law Explanations clearly describe the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Unless explicitly stated otherwise herein, this Confidentiality Agreement will terminate two (2) years from the date hereof and be governed by Texas law.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless explicitly stated otherwise herein, this Confidentiality Agreement will terminate when Daegis Inc. no longer sees a business need for its existence, regardless of the date hereof and be governed by Delaware law.
perturbation.explanation: The modification makes the termination date indefinite and dependent on the company's discretion, contradicting the original fixed term. It also changes the governing law from Texas to Delaware. An indefinite term may render the agreement unenforceable under Texas law, as it requires reasonably definite terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Contract Law - Definiteness of Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas contract law requires that a contract's essential terms be reasonably certain. An indefinite termination clause based solely on the company's subjective 'business need' may be considered too vague, rendering the agreement unenforceable in Texas. This contradicts the principle of definiteness required for contract formation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances which reasonably indicate that the goods are for the buyer, has made either a substantial beginning of their manufacture or commitments for their procurement; or (2) if the party against whom enforcement is sought admits in his pleading, testimony or otherwise in court that a contract for sale was made, but the contract is not enforceable under this provision beyond the quantity of goods admitted; or (3) with respect to goods for which payment has been made and accepted or which have been received and accepted (Section 2.606 ). Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.202. FINAL WRITTEN EXPRESSION: PAROL OR EXTRINSIC EVIDENCE. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (1) by course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade (Section 1.303 ); and (2) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Amended by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 542, Sec. 3, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. Sec. 2.203. SEALS INOPERATIVE. The affixing of a seal to a writing evidencing a contract for sale or an offer to buy or sell goods does not constitute the writing a sealed instrument and the law with respect to sealed instruments does not apply to such a contract or offer. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.204. FORMATION IN GENERAL. (a) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. (b) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (c) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.205. FIRM OFFERS. An offer by a merchant to buy or sell goods in a signed writing which by its terms gives assurance that it will be held open is not revocable, for lack of consideration, during the time stated or if no time is stated for a reasonable time, but in no event may such period of irrevocability exceed three months; but any such term of assurance on a form supplied by the offeree must be separately signed by the offeror. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.206. OFFER AND ACCEPTANCE IN FORMATION OF CONTRACT. (a) Unless otherwise unambiguously indicated by the language or circumstances (1) an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances; (2) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current shipment of conforming or non-conforming goods, but such a shipment of non-conforming goods does not constitute an acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer. (b) Where the beginning of a requested performance is a reasonable mode of acceptance an offeror who is not notified of acceptance within a reasonable time may treat the offer as h

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by making the termination clause indefinite based on the company's business needs, which may be considered too vague under Texas contract law. The original text specified a definite two-year term, whereas the changed text makes the contract's duration entirely dependent on the discretion of Daegis Inc. Additionally, the governing law is changed from Texas to Delaware. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses contract formation in general, stating in Sec. 2.204 (c) that a contract does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. However, the Law Explanation suggests that the new termination clause is too vague to be reasonably certain under Texas law, creating a contradiction because the contract's enforceability is now questionable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is irrelevant to the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: You understand and acknowledge that the Company and its Affiliates and representatives have made and make no representation hereunder, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Materials, expressly disclaim any and all liability for the information contained in or omitted from the Evaluation Material furnished by or on behalf of the Company and shall have no liability to you, your Affiliates or the Representatives or any other Person relating to or resulting from the use of the Evaluation Material or any errors therein or omissions therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: You understand and acknowledge that the Company and its Affiliates and representatives have made and make no representation hereunder, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Materials, expressly disclaim any and all liability for the information contained in or omitted from the Evaluation Material furnished by or on behalf of the Company, including liability for fraud.
perturbation.explanation: The modification adds 'including liability for fraud' to the disclaimer. A disclaimer for liability for fraud is generally unenforceable under Texas law, as public policy prevents parties from contracting away liability for intentional misconduct.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Law - Cannot Contract Away Liability for Fraud
perturbation.law_citation: Prudential Ins. Co. of America v. Jefferson Associates, Ltd., 896 S.W.2d 156 (Tex. 1995)
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law, as established in *Prudential Ins. Co. of America v. Jefferson Associates, Ltd.*, prohibits parties from contractually exempting themselves from liability for their own fraud. This modification directly contradicts this established legal principle, rendering the disclaimer unenforceable to the extent it attempts to shield the Company from liability for fraudulent misrepresentations or omissions.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds 'including liability for fraud' to the disclaimer, which attempts to shield the company from liability for fraudulent misrepresentations or omissions. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that Texas law prohibits parties from contracting away liability for their own fraud. This means the changed text directly contradicts established legal principles, making the disclaimer unenforceable regarding fraud. The addition of 'including liability for fraud' creates a contradiction because it attempts to waive liability for an action (fraud) that cannot be contractually waived under Texas law.
